items.title: U.S. indicts Cuba's Raúl Castro for murder over downing of planes in 1996
items.standfirst: Former Cuban leader Raúl Castro was indicted by a U.S. grand jury in connection with the Cuban military's fatal downing of two planes in 1996 — an escalation in the U.S. pressure campaign against the Cuban government.

He’ll do whatever I want him to do.”Trump also cited a poll that gave him 99% approval in Israel. Guardian US has not yet verified this poll. Continue reading...

items.title: Justice Department charges Raul Castro with murder
items.standfirst: A Florida grand jury indicted Raul Castro, the former leader of Cuba’s Communist regime, with murder and other charges for the 1996 shootdown of American humanitarian airplanes. NBC News Gabe Gutierrez reports.
items.content: A Florida grand jury indicted Raul Castro, the former leader of Cuba’s Communist regime, with murder and other charges for the 1996 shootdown of American humanitarian airplanes. NBC News Gabe Gutierrez reports.

items.title: US indicts former Cuban president Raúl Castro as it seeks to oust regime
items.standfirst: Charges filed in Miami against 94-year-old for allegedly shooting down exiles’ planes in 1996The United States issued a federal criminal indictment against Raúl Castro, Cuba’s former president, and five others on Wednesday in a significant escalation of the Trump administration’s campaign to oust the country’s six-decades-old communist regime.The 94-year-old political figurehead was charged in Miami, Florida, with conspiracy to kill US nationals, four counts of murder and two counts of destruction of aircraft. Continue reading...
items.content: Charges filed in Miami against 94-year-old for allegedly shooting down exiles’ planes in 1996The United States issued a federal criminal indictment against Raúl Castro, Cuba’s former president, and five others on Wednesday in a significant escalation of the Trump administration’s campaign to oust the country’s six-decades-old communist regime.The 94-year-old political figurehead was charged in Miami, Florida, with conspiracy to kill US nationals, four counts of murder and two counts of destruction of aircraft. Continue reading...

items.content: Cuba shot down two Brothers to the Rescue U.S. civilian planes in 1996, killing four Cuban Americans and drawing swift condemnation from the U.S. and the international community.

items.title: US announces charges against former Cuban leader Raúl Castro in 1996 downing of aircraft
items.standfirst: Federal prosecutors on Wednesday announced charges against former Cuban President Raúl Castro in the 1996 downing of civilian planes operated by Miami-based exiles as the Trump administration escalated pressure on the socialist government. FRANCE 24's Fraser Jackson reports from Washington.

items.title: Blanche faces questions over how Castro will stand trial in the U.S.
items.standfirst: Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche said that an arrest warrant has been issued for former Cuban leader Raúl Castro over a 1996 incident in which Cuban jets shot down two civilian planes, killing four Cuban Americans. NBC News’ Gabe Gutierrez reports on how Blanche responded to questions over how Castro is expected to stand trial in the U.S.
items.content: Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche said that an arrest warrant has been issued for former Cuban leader Raúl Castro over a 1996 incident in which Cuban jets shot down two civilian planes, killing four Cuban Americans. NBC News’ Gabe Gutierrez reports on how Blanche responded to questions over how Castro is expected to stand trial in the U.S.

items.title: The deadly plane attack at the centre of Castro's indictment
items.standfirst: Raúl Castro was armed forces minister when Cuban military jets shot down two civilian planes operated by exiles of the communist country.
items.content: Raúl Castro was armed forces minister when Cuban military jets shot down two civilian planes operated by exiles of the communist country.

items.title: US indicts Cuba's former president Raul Castro, 94, in connection with a 1996 incident
items.standfirst: The United States indicted former Cuban president Raul Castro in connection with a 1996 incident, Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche announced on Wednesday, marking ​an escalation in the pressure campaign against the communist government in Havana. Castro faces charges including murder, conspiracy to kill US nationals and destruction of aircraft.
items.content: The United States indicted former Cuban president Raul Castro in connection with a 1996 incident, Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche announced on Wednesday, marking ​an escalation in the pressure campaign against the communist government in Havana. Castro faces charges including murder, conspiracy to kill US nationals and destruction of aircraft.

items.content: Raul Castro, Cuba's former president, has been charged with murder in the US in connection with a military airstrike against two civilian planes, which killed four people.

items.title: Netanyahu scolds Israeli security minister for videos taunting flotilla activists
items.standfirst: Israel's national security minister triggered a backlash after releasing videos taunting detained flotilla activists who tried to breach the blockade of Gaza, telling them they should be imprisoned.
items.content: Israel's national security minister triggered a backlash after releasing videos taunting detained flotilla activists who tried to breach the blockade of Gaza, telling them they should be imprisoned.(Image credit: Ohad Zwigenberg)

items.title: Israeli security minister stirs diplomatic outrage with flotilla activist abuse video
items.standfirst: Far-right figure Itamar Ben-Gvir shares footage of himself taunting bound international detaineesIsrael’s far-right national security minister, Itamar Ben-Gvir, has sparked a diplomatic crisis by publishing footage of Israeli security forces abusing international activists who were detained as they tried to sail to Gaza with aid.Three activists were taken to hospital as result of Israeli violence, lawyers representing the group said. They were subsequently discharged. Dozens of others have suspected broken ribs, resulting in breathing problems. Continue reading...
items.content: Far-right figure Itamar Ben-Gvir shares footage of himself taunting bound international detaineesIsrael’s far-right national security minister, Itamar Ben-Gvir, has sparked a diplomatic crisis by publishing footage of Israeli security forces abusing international activists who were detained as they tried to sail to Gaza with aid.Three activists were taken to hospital as result of Israeli violence, lawyers representing the group said. They were subsequently discharged. Dozens of others have suspected broken ribs, resulting in breathing problems. Continue reading...

items.title: Israeli security minister taunts detained flotilla activists as police filmed forcing them to kneel
items.standfirst: Israeli police have been filmed forcing activists to kneel on the ground with their hands tied behind their backs while the country's national security minister tells them they should be imprisoned for a long time.
items.content: Israeli police have been filmed forcing activists to kneel on the ground with their hands tied behind their backs while the country's national security minister tells them they should be imprisoned for a long time.

items.title: Far-right Israeli minister sparks outcry with video of bound and kneeling Gaza flotilla activists
items.standfirst: Israel's far-right National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir posted a video on Wednesday of some 430 activists detained from the Gaza-bound Global Sumud Flotilla, many of them bound and kneeling, accompanied by the message: "Welcome to Israel." European governments and Israeli politicians were quick to denounce the video.
items.content: Israel's far-right National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir posted a video on Wednesday of some 430 activists detained from the Gaza-bound Global Sumud Flotilla, many of them bound and kneeling, accompanied by the message: "Welcome to Israel." European governments and Israeli politicians were quick to denounce the video.

clusters.title: Jan. 6 officer suing to block Trump fund: "These people... should not be rewarded by the government"
clusters.neutral_headline: US police officers sue Trump over $1.8bn ‘anti-weaponisation’ fund
bullet_summary: Reported by CBS News, Al Jazeera, France24

items.title: Jan. 6 officer suing to block Trump fund: "These people... should not be rewarded by the government"
items.standfirst: Metropolitan Police officer Daniel Hodges is one of two law enforcement officers who defended the Capitol during the Jan. 6 riots and is suing to block the Trump administration's nearly $1.8 billion so-called "anti-weaponization" fund. He joins "The Daily Report" to discuss why.
items.content: Metropolitan Police officer Daniel Hodges is one of two law enforcement officers who defended the Capitol during the Jan. 6 riots and is suing to block the Trump administration's nearly $1.8 billion so-called "anti-weaponization" fund. He joins "The Daily Report" to discuss why.

items.title: US police officers sue Trump over $1.8bn ‘anti-weaponisation’ fund
items.standfirst: Officers Harry Dunn and Daniel Hodges say the fund will likely be used to 'reward' rioters who attacked the US Capitol.
items.content: Officers Harry Dunn and Daniel Hodges say the fund will likely be used to 'reward' rioters who attacked the US Capitol.

items.title: Police sue to block Jan 6 rioters from payouts via Trump's 'Anti-Weaponization Fund'
items.standfirst: Two police officers who defended the US Capitol from a pro-Trump mob on January 6, 2021, filed suit Wednesday to block those who took part in the violence from receiving payouts from a new fund totaling nearly $1.8 billion. The fund, destined for Trump allies claiming political prosecution, was created this week in exchange for President Donald Trump dropping his $10 billion lawsuit against the IRS.
items.content: Two police officers who defended the US Capitol from a pro-Trump mob on January 6, 2021, filed suit Wednesday to block those who took part in the violence from receiving payouts from a new fund totaling nearly $1.8 billion. The fund, destined for Trump allies claiming political prosecution, was created this week in exchange for President Donald Trump dropping his $10 billion lawsuit against the IRS.

items.standfirst: The suspected death toll from the Ebola outbreak in central Africa rose again Wednesday as a group of potentially exposed Americans was arriving in Europe for monitoring and the World Health Organization warned a vaccine was still months away.
items.content: The suspected death toll from the Ebola outbreak in central Africa rose again Wednesday as a group of potentially exposed Americans was arriving in Europe for monitoring and the World Health Organization warned a vaccine was still months away.

items.title: Ebola deaths rise sharply as outbreak’s ‘scale and speed’ worry WHO chief
items.standfirst: The death toll from an Ebola outbreak in central Africa rose sharply Tuesday, with the head of the World Health Organization expressing concern over the “scale and speed of the epidemic.”At least 131 people are believed to have died and 531 suspected to have been infected, according to the Congolese Health Ministry.
items.content: The death toll from an Ebola outbreak in central Africa rose sharply Tuesday, with the head of the World Health Organization expressing concern over the “scale and speed of the epidemic.”At least 131 people are believed to have died and 531 suspected to have been infected, according to the Congolese Health Ministry.

But as Russia’s war in Ukraine makes Moscow increasingly dependent on China, and western leaders thaw relations with Beijing, what does the power imbalance mean for Xi and Putin’s relationship? items.title: Ex-DoJ prosecutor charged with sending sealed Jack Smith Trump report to personal email
items.standfirst: Carmen Mercedes Lineberger accused of hiding files related to Trump documents investigation as bundt cake recipeA former Department of Justice prosecutor is facing felony charges after emailing herself a sealed Biden-era investigative report concerning Donald Trump and attempting to hide the documents as cake recipes, federal authorities said on Wednesday.Carmen Mercedes Lineberger, who worked as a managing assistant US attorney in Florida, is facing two counts of theft of government money or property in addition to charges related to her alleged alteration of the documents, according to the indictment. Continue reading...

items.title: Middle East war live: US lifts sanctions against Francesca Albanese, UN expert on Palestinian rights
items.standfirst: The US removed Francesca Albanese – a UN ​expert on the Palestinian territories who has sharply criticised Israel – from its list of sanctioned individuals, according to the US Treasury Department website, following a court order. Follow our liveblog for the latest developments.
items.content: The US removed Francesca Albanese – a UN ​expert on the Palestinian territories who has sharply criticised Israel – from its list of sanctioned individuals, according to the US Treasury Department website, following a court order. Follow our liveblog for the latest developments.

items.content: Albanese is among several people sanctioned by the US for advocacy against Israel's genocidal war in Gaza.

items.title: US lifts sanctions against Francesca Albanese, UN rapporteur on Palestinians
items.standfirst: The United States on Wednesday lifted sanctions on Francesca Albanese, a UN expert critical of Israel’s treatment of Palestinians, after a court order. A Treasury Department notice showed Albanese was removed from a sanctions blacklist that had effectively cut her off from global banking and credit card services.
items.content: The United States on Wednesday lifted sanctions on Francesca Albanese, a UN expert critical of Israel’s treatment of Palestinians, after a court order. A Treasury Department notice showed Albanese was removed from a sanctions blacklist that had effectively cut her off from global banking and credit card services.

clusters.title: Beyond Plastics says tracked Starbucks cups didn’t reach a recycling facility
clusters.neutral_headline: Beyond Plastics says tracked Starbucks cups didn’t reach a recycling facility
bullet_summary: A three-month investigation found that none of the tracked Starbucks cold-beverage cups placed in in-store recycling bins ended up at a recycling facility
Reported by Reuters Top News, Guardian Business

items.title: Beyond Plastics says tracked Starbucks cups didn’t reach a recycling facility
items.standfirst: A three-month investigation found that none of the tracked Starbucks cold-beverage cups placed in in-store recycling bins ended up at a recycling facility. Instead, the cups were tracked to landfills, incinerators, waste-transfer stations, and material recovery facilities, raising questions about Starbucks’ “widely recyclable” claim. [beyondplastics.org]
items.content: A three-month investigation found that none of the tracked Starbucks cold-beverage cups placed in in-store recycling bins ended up at a recycling facility. Instead, the cups were tracked to landfills, incinerators, waste-transfer stations, and material recovery facilities, raising questions about Starbucks’ “widely recyclable” claim. [beyondplastics.org]

items.title: Starbucks does not recycle plastic cups it claims are ‘widely recyclable’, report says
items.standfirst: Watchdog group glued trackers to 53 of the chain’s cups across nine states and found none ended up at a recycling facilityIf you attach a GPS tracker to a “widely recyclable” plastic Starbucks cup and drop it in an in-store recycling bin, you might expect it to end up in a recycling plant, but the environmental watchdog organization Beyond Plastics says that’s not the case in a new report.Starbucks announced that their plastic cups were now considered “widely recyclable” earlier this year, according to How2Recycle, a group affiliated with the consumer packaging industry that helps private companies label their packaging with recycling options. The coffee giant touted the achievement as a “big milestone, with huge impact”. Continue reading...
items.content: Watchdog group glued trackers to 53 of the chain’s cups across nine states and found none ended up at a recycling facilityIf you attach a GPS tracker to a “widely recyclable” plastic Starbucks cup and drop it in an in-store recycling bin, you might expect it to end up in a recycling plant, but the environmental watchdog organization Beyond Plastics says that’s not the case in a new report.Starbucks announced that their plastic cups were now considered “widely recyclable” earlier this year, according to How2Recycle, a group affiliated with the consumer packaging industry that helps private companies label their packaging with recycling options. The coffee giant touted the achievement as a “big milestone, with huge impact”. Continue reading...
